meta.notes: File 04: OpenStax Microbiology Ch. 6 (Viruses), Ch. 17 (Innate Nonspecific Host Defenses), and Ch. 18 (Adaptive Specific Host Defenses). One 'select all that apply' item (Ch.6 Q2) and matching/ordering items (Ch.17 Q17-18, Ch.18 Q19-20) were skipped. Options 1-4 map to A-D; correctAnswer letters verified against the chapter answer keys.

questions.stem: The component(s) of a virus that is/are extended from the envelope for attachment is/are the:
questions.explanation: Glycoprotein spikes project from the viral envelope and mediate attachment to host-cell receptors.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q1 = B.

hints.prompt: What is the general role of the glycoprotein projections on an enveloped virus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They attach the virus to receptors on the host cell

choices.label: C
choices.text: They form the innermost core of the virus

choices.label: D
choices.text: They provide energy for the virus

hints.rationale: Surface projections on an enveloped virus function in attaching to host-cell receptors.

hints.prompt: In this question, what structure are 'capsomeres' the building blocks of?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The protein capsid that encloses the genome

choices.label: D
choices.text: The attachment projections on the envelope surface

hints.rationale: Capsomeres are protein subunits of the capsid, an internal shell rather than an external projection.

hints.prompt: Why is 'nucleic acid' NOT the component extended from the envelope for attachment?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is the internal genome carried inside the capsid, not an external projection

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a lipid that coats the envelope

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is a receptor found only on host cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is the same thing as a capsomere

hints.rationale: The nucleic acid is the internal genetic material, so it cannot be the outward attachment structure.

questions.stem: The envelope of a virus is derived from the host's
questions.explanation: Enveloped viruses acquire their lipid envelope from host membrane structures (such as the plasma membrane) as they bud from the cell.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q3 = B.

hints.prompt: How does an enveloped virus typically acquire its lipid envelope?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By taking a piece of a host membrane as it buds out

choices.label: B
choices.text: By synthesizing lipids from its own genome

choices.label: C
choices.text: By stealing ribosomes from the host

choices.label: D
choices.text: By copying host DNA into lipids

hints.rationale: Enveloped viruses derive their envelope from host membranes during budding.

hints.prompt: The viral envelope is chemically a lipid bilayer, so which host component could supply it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A membrane structure such as the plasma membrane

hints.rationale: Only a lipid-bilayer membrane can donate material chemically like the envelope.

hints.prompt: Why can 'nucleic acids' and 'genome' be ruled out as the envelope source?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Both are genetic material, not the lipid membrane that forms an envelope

choices.label: B
choices.text: Both are lipids that make membranes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Both are proteins in the capsid

hints.rationale: Nucleic acids and the genome are genetic material, chemically unlike a lipid envelope.

questions.stem: In naming viruses, the family name ends with ________ and genus name ends with _________.
questions.explanation: In virus taxonomy, family names end in -viridae and genus names end in -virus.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax (en-dashes normalized to hyphens). correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q4 = B.

hints.prompt: In virus taxonomy, which suffix marks a virus FAMILY name?
hints.rationale: Virus family names end in -viridae.

hints.prompt: Which suffix marks a virus GENUS name?
hints.rationale: Virus genus names end in -virus.

hints.prompt: Why can the options containing 'virion' be eliminated?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'Virion' refers to a complete virus particle, not a taxonomic-rank suffix

hints.rationale: A virion is a whole particle, so any option using it as a rank suffix is wrong.

questions.stem: What is another name for a nonenveloped virus?
questions.explanation: A virus lacking a lipid envelope (only a capsid around its genome) is called a naked virus.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q5 = C.

hints.prompt: What does it mean for a virus to be 'nonenveloped'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It has only a capsid around its genome, with no lipid envelope

hints.rationale: Nonenveloped means lacking a lipid envelope, leaving just the capsid.

hints.prompt: Terms like 'provirus' and 'latent virus' describe what about a virus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A dormant or integrated genome state, not its structural covering

choices.label: B
choices.text: The number of envelopes it has

hints.rationale: Those terms describe life-cycle/genome states, not whether an envelope is present.

hints.prompt: Why is 'provirus' NOT another name for a nonenveloped virus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A provirus is viral DNA integrated into the host genome, unrelated to the envelope

choices.label: B
choices.text: A provirus is a virus with an extra envelope

choices.label: C
choices.text: A provirus lacks a genome entirely

choices.label: D
choices.text: A provirus is identical to a naked virus

hints.rationale: Provirus refers to integrated viral DNA, a state that has nothing to do with lacking an envelope.

questions.stem: Which of the following leads to the destruction of the host cells?
questions.explanation: The lytic cycle ends with assembly of new virions and lysis (rupture) of the host cell. The lysogenic cycle integrates the phage genome without immediately killing the cell.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q6 = B.

hints.prompt: What key outcome distinguishes the lytic cycle from the lysogenic cycle?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The lytic cycle ends by bursting (lysing) and killing the host cell

choices.label: B
choices.text: The lytic cycle silently integrates viral DNA

choices.label: C
choices.text: The lytic cycle never makes new virions

choices.label: D
choices.text: The lytic cycle protects the host cell

hints.rationale: The lytic cycle culminates in host-cell lysis and death.

hints.prompt: During the lysogenic cycle, the phage genome mainly does what to the host cell?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Integrates and replicates with the host without immediately killing it

choices.label: C
choices.text: Digests the host membrane at once

hints.rationale: Lysogeny integrates the phage DNA and spares the cell for the time being.

hints.prompt: Why is 'prophage' not the answer to what destroys host cells?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A prophage is dormant phage DNA integrated in the host, not an active cell-killing process

choices.label: B
choices.text: A prophage instantly lyses the cell

choices.label: C
choices.text: A prophage is a host digestive enzyme

hints.rationale: A prophage is an integrated, dormant genome, so it does not itself destroy the cell.

questions.stem: A virus obtains its envelope during which of the following phases?
questions.explanation: Enveloped viruses typically gain their envelope by budding through a host membrane during the release phase.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q7 = D.

hints.prompt: By what process do most enveloped viruses obtain their envelope?
hints.rationale: Enveloped viruses gain their envelope by budding through a host membrane.

hints.prompt: Budding through the host membrane happens as the new virus is doing what?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Being assembled inside the nucleus only

hints.rationale: Budding coincides with the virus exiting the cell.

hints.prompt: Why is 'penetration' not when the envelope is acquired?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Penetration is viral entry into the cell, whereas the envelope is gained on the way out

choices.label: B
choices.text: Penetration is the last step of infection

choices.label: C
choices.text: Penetration strips off the capsid permanently

hints.rationale: Penetration is an entry step, so it is not when an exiting virus wraps itself in membrane.

questions.stem: Which of the following components is brought into a cell by HIV?
questions.explanation: HIV is a retrovirus that carries reverse transcriptase, which synthesizes DNA from its RNA genome inside the host cell.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q8 = D.

hints.prompt: What enzyme is the hallmark of a retrovirus?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reverse transcriptase, which makes DNA from an RNA template

choices.label: C
choices.text: DNA ligase that seals host DNA

hints.rationale: Retroviruses characteristically carry reverse transcriptase (RNA-dependent DNA polymerase).

hints.prompt: HIV enters the cell carrying which type of genome?
hints.rationale: HIV is a retrovirus that carries an RNA genome.

hints.prompt: Why can 'a DNA-dependent DNA polymerase' be ruled out for HIV?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It needs a DNA template, but HIV enters with RNA, so it could not start replication

choices.label: B
choices.text: HIV carries no genetic material to copy

choices.label: C
choices.text: A DNA-dependent DNA polymerase actually makes RNA

hints.rationale: With only an RNA genome on entry, a DNA-templated polymerase would have nothing to copy.

questions.stem: A positive-strand RNA virus:

choices.label: A
choices.text: must first be converted to a mRNA before it can be translated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: can be used directly to translate viral proteins.

choices.label: C
choices.text: will be degraded by host enzymes.

choices.label: D
choices.text: is not recognized by host ribosomes.

questions.explanation: A positive-sense (+) RNA genome reads like mRNA, so host ribosomes can translate it directly into viral proteins.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q9 = B.

hints.prompt: What does 'positive-sense (+) RNA' mean for a viral genome?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It reads like mRNA and can be translated directly

choices.label: B
choices.text: It must always be copied into DNA first

choices.label: C
choices.text: It cannot be read by any ribosome

hints.rationale: Positive-sense RNA has the same sequence sense as mRNA.

hints.prompt: Given that +RNA reads like mRNA, what can host ribosomes do with it?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Translate it into viral proteins right away

choices.label: D
choices.text: Package it into a capsid without reading it

hints.rationale: Host ribosomes can translate a molecule that reads like mRNA.

hints.prompt: Why is 'must first be converted to mRNA before it can be translated' incorrect here?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Positive-sense RNA already functions as mRNA, so no prior conversion is needed

choices.label: B
choices.text: Positive-sense RNA is a DNA molecule

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ribosomes cannot read any form of RNA

choices.label: D
choices.text: That statement defines a positive-sense virus

hints.rationale: Because +RNA is already mRNA-like, the conversion step described does not apply.

questions.stem: What is the name for the transfer of genetic information from one bacterium to another bacterium by a phage?
questions.explanation: Transduction is the bacteriophage-mediated transfer of bacterial DNA from one cell to another.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q10 = A.

hints.prompt: What is transduction in bacteria?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Transfer of bacterial DNA from one cell to another by a bacteriophage

choices.label: B
choices.text: Uptake of naked DNA from the environment

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct cell-to-cell contact through a pilus

hints.rationale: Transduction is phage-mediated transfer of bacterial DNA.

hints.prompt: For gene transfer 'by a phage' specifically, what carries the DNA between cells?

choices.label: C
choices.text: A plasmid moving through a pilus

hints.rationale: The defining feature here is that a phage vehicle carries the DNA.

hints.prompt: Why is 'translation' not the term for phage-mediated DNA transfer?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Translation is protein synthesis from mRNA, not movement of DNA between cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Translation physically carries DNA between bacteria

choices.label: C
choices.text: Translation is performed only by phages

choices.label: D
choices.text: Translation integrates phage DNA into the chromosome

hints.rationale: Translation makes protein and does not transfer DNA between cells.

questions.stem: Which of the followings cannot be used to culture viruses?
questions.explanation: Viruses are obligate intracellular parasites and require living host cells; they cannot be grown in a cell-free liquid medium alone.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q11 = B.

hints.prompt: Why are viruses called obligate intracellular parasites?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They can replicate only inside living host cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: They grow best in plain nutrient broth

choices.label: C
choices.text: They reproduce on any moist surface

choices.label: D
choices.text: They make their own energy independently

hints.rationale: Viruses require living host cells to replicate.

hints.prompt: What do tissue culture, embryos, and animal hosts all provide that supports viral growth?
hints.rationale: Each of those methods supplies living cells for the virus.

hints.prompt: Why is 'embryo' NOT a correct choice for something that cannot culture viruses?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Embryonated eggs contain living cells that can support viral replication

choices.label: D
choices.text: Embryos destroy every virus on contact

hints.rationale: An embryo provides living cells, so it can culture viruses and is not the exception.

questions.stem: Which of the following tests can be used to detect the presence of a specific virus?
questions.explanation: Enzyme immunoassay (EIA), RT-PCR, and PCR can all be used to detect specific viruses, so all of the above is correct.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q12 = D.

hints.prompt: EIA, PCR, and RT-PCR are all examples of what kind of technique?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Laboratory assays that can detect a specific virus

choices.label: B
choices.text: Methods for growing viruses in eggs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ways to visualize viruses in a light microscope

hints.rationale: Each is a diagnostic assay capable of detecting a specific virus.

hints.prompt: The reverse-transcription step of RT-PCR specifically enables detection of what?
hints.rationale: RT-PCR reverse-transcribes RNA, letting it detect RNA viruses, confirming it is a real detection method.

hints.prompt: Why would answering with a single method such as 'RT-PCR' alone be a weak choice?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The other listed assays also detect viruses, so one method alone may be incomplete

hints.rationale: Since more than one listed assay independently detects viruses, choosing just one overlooks the others.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a cytopathic effect?
questions.explanation: A normal mononucleated cell is not a sign of viral damage. Transformation, cell fusion (syncytia), and inclusion bodies are all cytopathic effects.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q13 = C.

hints.prompt: What is a cytopathic effect (CPE)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A visible virus-induced change or damage to the host cell

choices.label: B
choices.text: A normal, healthy feature of an uninfected cell

hints.rationale: A CPE is an observable change in a cell caused by viral infection.

hints.prompt: Viral cell fusion produces syncytia (multinucleated cells); how many nuclei does a normal, unfused cell have?
hints.rationale: A normal mononucleated cell has a single nucleus, which is not a sign of viral damage.

hints.prompt: Why is 'transformation' actually a cytopathic effect (so not the exception asked for)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is a virus-induced change that can push a cell toward a cancer-like state

choices.label: B
choices.text: It means the cell stays perfectly normal

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is ordinary, healthy cell division

choices.label: D
choices.text: It has nothing to do with viruses

hints.rationale: Transformation is a genuine virus-caused change, so it is a CPE rather than the odd one out.

questions.stem: Which of these infectious agents do not have nucleic acid?
questions.explanation: Prions are infectious proteins and contain no nucleic acid. Viroids (RNA), viruses, and bacteria all contain nucleic acid.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q14 = D.

hints.prompt: What are prions composed of?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Protein only, with no nucleic acid

hints.rationale: Prions are infectious proteins containing no nucleic acid.

hints.prompt: Do viruses and bacteria contain nucleic acid?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Yes, both carry DNA or RNA as genetic material

choices.label: B
choices.text: No, neither has any nucleic acid

choices.label: C
choices.text: Only viruses do; bacteria do not

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only bacteria do; viruses do not

hints.rationale: Both viruses and bacteria possess nucleic-acid genomes, so they are not the 'no nucleic acid' answer.

hints.prompt: Why do viroids NOT belong to the 'no nucleic acid' group?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Viroids are small circular RNA molecules, so they do contain nucleic acid

choices.label: B
choices.text: Viroids are made purely of lipid

choices.label: C
choices.text: Viroids have no genetic material at all

choices.label: D
choices.text: Viroids are a kind of bacterium

hints.rationale: A viroid is composed of RNA, which is a nucleic acid.

questions.stem: Which of the following is true of prions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They can be inactivated by boiling at 100 °C.

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are a rogue form of protein, PrP.

choices.label: D
choices.text: They can be reliably inactivated by an autoclave.

questions.explanation: Prions are a misfolded (rogue) form of the normal prion protein PrP. They are remarkably resistant to boiling and standard autoclaving and have no capsid.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 6 Viruses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-6), Ch.6 MC Q15 = C.

hints.prompt: What is a prion, fundamentally?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A misfolded (rogue) form of a normal cellular protein

hints.rationale: A prion is a misfolded form of the normal prion protein.

hints.prompt: How resistant are prions to boiling and standard autoclaving?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Highly resistant; ordinary heat sterilization does not reliably destroy them

choices.label: B
choices.text: Extremely fragile; a few seconds of warmth destroys them

choices.label: C
choices.text: Destroyed instantly by boiling at 100 degrees C

hints.rationale: Prions are notably resistant to heat, so claims of easy heat inactivation are false.

hints.prompt: Why is 'They contain a capsid' incorrect for prions?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Prions are just misfolded protein with no capsid or nucleic acid to enclose

choices.label: B
choices.text: Prions have a thick lipid capsid

choices.label: D
choices.text: Prions are enveloped in two capsids

hints.rationale: A capsid encloses a genome, but prions have no genome and no capsid.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the innate nonspecific immune system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a targeted and highly specific response to a single pathogen or molecule

choices.label: B
choices.text: a generalized and nonspecific set of defenses against a class or group of pathogens

choices.label: C
choices.text: a set of barrier mechanisms that adapts to specific pathogens after repeated exposure

choices.label: D
choices.text: the production of antibody molecules against pathogens

questions.explanation: Innate immunity provides rapid, generalized, nonspecific defense against broad classes of pathogens, without the antigen-specific memory of adaptive immunity.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 17 Innate Nonspecific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-17), Ch.17 MC Q1 = B.

hints.prompt: How is the innate immune system best characterized compared with adaptive immunity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A rapid, generalized, nonspecific defense against broad groups of pathogens

choices.label: B
choices.text: A slow response that targets one specific molecule

choices.label: C
choices.text: A memory-based response built after repeated exposure

choices.label: D
choices.text: A system based entirely on antibody production

hints.rationale: Innate immunity is fast, broad, and nonspecific.

hints.prompt: Which feature belongs to ADAPTIVE immunity rather than innate immunity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Highly specific targeting of a single pathogen with memory

hints.rationale: Specific, memory-based targeting is a mark of adaptive, not innate, immunity.

hints.prompt: Why is 'a targeted, highly specific response to a single pathogen' wrong for the innate system?

choices.label: A
choices.text: That describes adaptive immunity; innate defenses are broad and nonspecific

choices.label: B
choices.text: Innate immunity attacks exactly one pathogen at a time

hints.rationale: Specificity for a single pathogen is an adaptive trait, so it cannot define innate immunity.

questions.stem: Which of the following constantly sheds dead cells along with any microbes that may be attached to those cells?
questions.explanation: The epidermis continually sheds its outermost dead keratinized cells, carrying away attached microbes as a physical defense.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 17 Innate Nonspecific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-17), Ch.17 MC Q2 = A.

hints.prompt: How does the outermost layer of skin act as a physical barrier to microbes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It continually sheds dead surface cells, carrying attached microbes away

choices.label: B
choices.text: It secretes antibodies into the blood

hints.rationale: Shedding of dead outer cells removes attached microbes.

hints.prompt: Where are the dermis and hypodermis located relative to the skin surface?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deeper, beneath the surface layer, so they do not shed to the outside

choices.label: B
choices.text: They are the outermost exposed layers

choices.label: C
choices.text: They float on top of the epidermis

hints.rationale: The dermis and hypodermis are deep layers, not the shedding surface.

hints.prompt: Why is the 'dermis' not the layer that sheds dead cells with attached microbes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The dermis is a deeper connective layer, not the exposed shedding surface

choices.label: B
choices.text: The dermis is the outermost dead layer

choices.label: C
choices.text: The dermis is made of keratinized dead cells that flake off

choices.label: D
choices.text: The dermis faces the outside environment directly

hints.rationale: Because the dermis lies beneath the surface, it is not the layer that continually sheds.

questions.stem: Which of the following uses a particularly dense suite of tight junctions to prevent microbes from entering the underlying tissue?
questions.explanation: The blood-brain barrier relies on especially tight junctions between capillary endothelial cells to exclude microbes and many molecules from the central nervous system.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 17 Innate Nonspecific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-17), Ch.17 MC Q3 = C.

hints.prompt: What do tight junctions between cells accomplish?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They seal neighboring cells together to block passage between them

choices.label: B
choices.text: They pump mucus across a surface

hints.rationale: Tight junctions seal cells so substances and microbes cannot slip between them.

hints.prompt: A barrier protecting the central nervous system would rely most on which feature?

choices.label: A
choices.text: An especially dense set of tight junctions

hints.rationale: Protecting the CNS depends on unusually tight junctions between capillary cells.

hints.prompt: Why is the 'mucociliary escalator' not the tight-junction barrier described?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It clears microbes using mucus and beating cilia, not a dense tight-junction seal

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is located inside the brain

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is built purely from tight junctions

choices.label: D
choices.text: It guards entry into the spinal cord

hints.rationale: The mucociliary escalator works by mucus and cilia, a different mechanism than tight junctions.

questions.stem: Which of the following serve as chemical signals between cells and stimulate a wide range of nonspecific defenses?
questions.explanation: Cytokines are signaling proteins released by cells that coordinate and stimulate many nonspecific (and specific) immune defenses.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 17 Innate Nonspecific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-17), Ch.17 MC Q4 = A.

hints.prompt: What are cytokines?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Signaling proteins that carry messages between cells to coordinate defenses

choices.label: B
choices.text: Enzymes that digest the host cell wall

hints.rationale: Cytokines are chemical messengers between cells.

hints.prompt: Compared with signaling molecules, how do antimicrobial peptides and complement mainly act?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They directly attack or kill microbes rather than serving as cell-to-cell signals

choices.label: B
choices.text: They only carry messages between cells

hints.rationale: AMPs and complement are direct effectors, not primarily signaling molecules.

hints.prompt: Why are 'antibodies' not the best answer for nonspecific cell-signaling molecules?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antibodies are specific products of adaptive immunity, not general nonspecific signals

choices.label: B
choices.text: Antibodies are a type of cytokine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antibodies signal nonspecifically between all cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Antibodies are not part of immunity

hints.rationale: Antibodies are specific adaptive molecules, so they do not fit a nonspecific signaling role.

questions.stem: Bacteriocins and defensins are types of which of the following?

choices.text: MHC I is missing from most cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: MHC I is a secreted antibody

hints.rationale: MHC I is broadly expressed, so it is not the APC-restricted molecule.

questions.stem: What is a superantigen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: a protein that is highly efficient at stimulating a single type of productive and specific T cell response

choices.label: B
choices.text: a protein produced by antigen-presenting cells to enhance their presentation capabilities

choices.label: C
choices.text: a protein produced by T cells as a way of increasing the antigen activation they receive from antigen-presenting cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: a protein that activates T cells in a nonspecific and uncontrolled manner

questions.explanation: A superantigen cross-links MHC II and T-cell receptors outside the normal antigen groove, activating large numbers of T cells nonspecifically and triggering a massive, harmful cytokine release.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 18 Adaptive Specific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-18), Ch.18 MC Q9 = D.

hints.prompt: What is the defining action of a superantigen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It activates large numbers of T cells nonspecifically, causing massive cytokine release

choices.label: B
choices.text: It carefully activates one specific T-cell clone

choices.label: C
choices.text: It shuts down all T cells permanently

choices.label: D
choices.text: It converts T cells into B cells

hints.rationale: A superantigen nonspecifically activates many T cells at once.

hints.prompt: Does a superantigen work through the normal, precise antigen-recognition process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; it cross-links MHC II and TCRs outside the normal antigen groove

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; it fits neatly into one specific TCR groove

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes; it requires exact epitope matching

choices.label: D
choices.text: No; it avoids T cells entirely

hints.rationale: Superantigens bypass normal specific recognition, activating T cells indiscriminately.

hints.prompt: Why is 'highly efficient at stimulating a single, specific T-cell response' the opposite of a superantigen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Superantigens activate many T cells nonspecifically, not one specific clone

choices.label: B
choices.text: Superantigens target exactly one antigen with precision

choices.label: C
choices.text: Superantigens are extremely specific by design

hints.rationale: A superantigen is nonspecific, so a single precise response describes the opposite behavior.

questions.stem: To what does the TCR of a helper T cell bind?

choices.label: A
choices.text: antigens presented with MHC I molecules

choices.label: B
choices.text: antigens presented with MHC II molecules

choices.label: C
choices.text: free antigen in a soluble form

questions.explanation: Helper T cells (CD4+) recognize antigens presented on MHC class II molecules by antigen-presenting cells.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 18 Adaptive Specific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-18), Ch.18 MC Q10 = B.

hints.prompt: Helper T cells (CD4+) recognize antigen presented on which MHC class?

choices.label: C
choices.text: No MHC; they bind free antigen

hints.rationale: Helper (CD4) T cells recognize antigen on MHC II.

hints.prompt: Can a T-cell receptor recognize free, soluble antigen floating in solution?

choices.label: A
choices.text: No; the TCR recognizes peptide presented by an MHC molecule

choices.label: B
choices.text: Yes; TCRs bind any free antigen directly

choices.label: C
choices.text: Yes; but only if the antigen is a lipid

choices.label: D
choices.text: No; TCRs bind only whole cells

hints.rationale: TCRs require MHC-presented peptide, not free antigen.

hints.prompt: Why is 'antigens presented with MHC I' wrong for a helper T cell?

choices.label: A
choices.text: MHC I is recognized by cytotoxic (CD8) T cells, not helper (CD4) T cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Helper T cells ignore MHC entirely

choices.label: C
choices.text: MHC I presents specifically to helper T cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Helper T cells bind only free antigen

hints.rationale: MHC I engages cytotoxic T cells, so it is not the ligand for helper T cells.

questions.stem: Cytotoxic T cells will bind with their TCR to which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: antigens presented with MHC I molecules

choices.label: B
choices.text: antigens presented with MHC II molecules

choices.label: C
choices.text: free antigen in a soluble form

questions.explanation: Cytotoxic T cells (CD8+) recognize antigens presented on MHC class I molecules, enabling them to kill infected or abnormal cells.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 18 Adaptive Specific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-18), Ch.18 MC Q11 = A.

hints.prompt: Cytotoxic T cells (CD8+) recognize antigen presented on which MHC class?

choices.label: C
choices.text: No MHC; they bind free antigen

hints.rationale: Cytotoxic (CD8) T cells recognize antigen on MHC I.

hints.prompt: Does the cytotoxic T-cell receptor bind soluble antigen or MHC-presented peptide?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Free soluble antigen in the plasma

hints.rationale: The TCR binds peptide displayed on MHC, not free antigen.

hints.prompt: Why is 'antigens presented with MHC II' wrong for a cytotoxic T cell?

choices.label: A
choices.text: MHC II is recognized by helper (CD4) T cells, not cytotoxic (CD8) T cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cytotoxic T cells only ever see MHC II

choices.label: C
choices.text: MHC II is present on all nucleated cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Cytotoxic T cells bind only soluble antigen

hints.rationale: MHC II engages helper T cells, so it is not the ligand for cytotoxic T cells.

questions.stem: A ________ molecule is a glycoprotein used to identify and distinguish white blood cells.
questions.explanation: Cluster of differentiation (CD) molecules are cell-surface glycoproteins used as markers to identify and distinguish types of white blood cells (e.g., CD4, CD8).
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 18 Adaptive Specific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-18), Ch.18 MC Q12 = D.

hints.prompt: What are cluster of differentiation (CD) molecules used for?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Surface markers to identify and distinguish types of white blood cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fibers that give cells their shape

hints.rationale: CD molecules are surface markers used to classify leukocytes.

hints.prompt: Are the T-cell receptor and B-cell receptor used mainly as general identification markers or as antigen receptors?

choices.label: A
choices.text: As antigen receptors that recognize specific antigens

choices.label: B
choices.text: As universal ID tags for all leukocytes

hints.rationale: TCR and BCR are antigen receptors, not general leukocyte ID markers.

hints.prompt: Why is 'MHC I' not the glycoprotein used as a marker to distinguish white blood cell types?

choices.label: A
choices.text: MHC I presents antigen on nucleated cells; leukocyte subsets are distinguished by CD markers

choices.label: B
choices.text: MHC I is a unique ID tag for each leukocyte type

choices.label: C
choices.text: MHC I is not a glycoprotein

choices.label: D
choices.text: MHC I is found only on white blood cells

hints.rationale: MHC I functions in antigen presentation, whereas CD molecules are the leukocyte-distinguishing markers.

questions.stem: Name the T helper cell subset involved in antibody production.
questions.explanation: TH2 helper T cells secrete cytokines that drive B-cell activation and antibody production (humoral immunity), whereas TH1 cells favor cell-mediated responses.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 18 Adaptive Specific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-18), Ch.18 MC Q13 = B.

hints.prompt: Which helper T-cell subset mainly promotes antibody (humoral) responses by helping B cells?
hints.rationale: TH2 cells promote B-cell activation and antibody production.

hints.prompt: What type of immunity do TH1 cells primarily favor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Cell-mediated responses such as macrophage activation

hints.rationale: TH1 cells drive cell-mediated immunity, contrasting with antibody-focused help.

hints.prompt: Why is 'TH1' not the subset that drives antibody production?

choices.label: A
choices.text: TH1 cells promote cell-mediated immunity, not primarily antibody production

choices.label: B
choices.text: TH1 cells cannot secrete any cytokines

choices.label: C
choices.text: TH1 cells are actually B cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: TH1 cells directly secrete antibodies themselves

hints.rationale: TH1 favors cell-mediated responses, so it is not the antibody-promoting helper subset.

questions.stem: Which of the following would be a T-dependent antigen?
questions.explanation: T-dependent antigens are typically proteins, which can be processed and presented on MHC II to recruit helper T-cell assistance for B-cell responses.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 18 Adaptive Specific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-18), Ch.18 MC Q14 = C.

hints.prompt: What makes an antigen 'T-dependent'?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It can be processed and presented on MHC II to recruit helper T-cell assistance

choices.label: B
choices.text: It never needs any immune cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is always a simple sugar

choices.label: D
choices.text: It only activates red blood cells

hints.rationale: T-dependent antigens can be presented on MHC II to enlist T-cell help.

hints.prompt: Which class of molecule can be processed into peptides and displayed on MHC II?
hints.rationale: Only peptides (from proteins) are presented on MHC II to recruit T cells.

hints.prompt: Why is 'lipopolysaccharide' typically a T-INdependent rather than T-dependent antigen?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is not a peptide, so it cannot be presented on MHC II to recruit T-cell help

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is a protein readily processed by APCs

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is displayed on MHC II like a peptide

choices.label: D
choices.text: It requires helper T cells before any response

hints.rationale: Because LPS is not a peptide, it cannot engage the MHC II/T-helper pathway.

questions.stem: Which of the following would be a BCR?
questions.explanation: The B-cell receptor (BCR) is a membrane-bound immunoglobulin; naive B cells display membrane IgM and IgD as their BCR.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 18 Adaptive Specific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-18), Ch.18 MC Q15 = D.

hints.prompt: What is the B-cell receptor (BCR) chemically?
hints.rationale: The BCR is a membrane-bound immunoglobulin.

hints.prompt: Which membrane immunoglobulins serve as the BCR on a naive B cell?
hints.rationale: Naive B cells display membrane IgM and IgD as their BCR.

hints.prompt: Why is 'MHC II' not a B-cell receptor?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The BCR is a membrane immunoglobulin, whereas MHC II is a separate antigen-presenting molecule

choices.label: B
choices.text: MHC II is a membrane-bound antibody

choices.label: C
choices.text: MHC II binds antigen the way a BCR does

choices.label: D
choices.text: MHC II is another name for IgD

hints.rationale: MHC II presents antigen but is not an immunoglobulin, so it is not the BCR.

questions.stem: Which of the following does not occur during the lag period of the primary antibody response?

choices.label: C
choices.text: presentation of antigen with MHC II

questions.explanation: During the lag phase, antigen is recognized and presented and T cells are activated, but class switching to IgG occurs later; the initial antibody made is IgM.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 18 Adaptive Specific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-18), Ch.18 MC Q16 = B.

hints.prompt: What generally happens during the lag period of a primary antibody response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Antigen is recognized and presented and T cells are activated, before much antibody is made

choices.label: B
choices.text: Large amounts of high-affinity IgG are immediately secreted

choices.label: D
choices.text: Memory cells are already producing IgG in bulk

hints.rationale: The lag phase involves recognition, presentation, and T-cell activation with little antibody yet.

hints.prompt: Which antibody isotype is produced FIRST in a primary response?
hints.rationale: IgM appears first; switching to IgG comes later, not during the initial lag.

hints.prompt: Why is 'activation of helper T cells' NOT the correct choice (since it does occur during the lag period)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: T-cell activation happens early in the lag phase, so it is not the event excluded from it

choices.label: B
choices.text: Helper T cells are never activated at all

choices.label: C
choices.text: It occurs only after antibody levels peak

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is identical to class switching

hints.rationale: Helper T-cell activation is part of the lag period, so it is not the event that fails to occur then.

questions.stem: A patient is bitten by a dog with confirmed rabies infection. After treating the bite wound, the physician injects the patient with antibodies that are specific for the rabies virus to prevent the development of an active infection. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Receiving pre-formed antibodies by injection is passive immunity, and because it is delivered medically it is artificial — hence artificial passive immunity.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 18 Adaptive Specific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-18), Ch.18 MC Q17 = D.

hints.prompt: Receiving pre-formed antibodies (rather than making your own) is which kind of immunity?
hints.rationale: Getting ready-made antibodies is passive immunity.

hints.prompt: When immunity is delivered by a medical treatment such as an injection, it is termed:
hints.rationale: Medically delivered immunity is artificial, as opposed to naturally acquired.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be ruled out because the patient did NOT mount his own immune response?
hints.rationale: Because pre-formed antibodies were given (passive), both 'active' options can be eliminated.

questions.stem: A patient gets a cold, and recovers a few days later. The patient's classmates come down with the same cold roughly a week later, but the original patient does not get the same cold again. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Mounting one's own immune response after a natural infection produces natural active immunity, including memory that protects against reinfection.
questions.sourceName: OpenStax Microbiology, Ch. 18 Adaptive Specific Host Defenses — Review Questions (Multiple Choice)
questions.notes: Stem/options verbatim from OpenStax. correctAnswer from OpenStax Answer Key (pages/chapter-18), Ch.18 MC Q18 = A.

hints.prompt: Mounting your OWN immune response after an infection produces which kind of immunity?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Purely innate immunity with no memory

hints.rationale: Making your own response after infection is active immunity.

hints.prompt: When immunity is gained through everyday infection rather than a medical product, it is termed:
hints.rationale: Immunity from a natural infection is naturally acquired.

hints.prompt: Which two options can be ruled out because the person received no external antibodies (they made their own)?
hints.rationale: Since the person fought off the infection themselves (active), both 'passive' options can be eliminated.
